beats.text: Dynamic programming could solve any problem, but only if you handed it the transition probabilities and rewards in advance.

visual.sub: Drop the model, keep the goal

beats.text: Real agents are never handed the model, so model-free methods replace the expectation over next states with sampled experience.

visual.note: Average enough samples and the law of large numbers recovers the expectation.

beats.text: Instead of asking for the average outcome, the agent takes the action, sees one outcome, and nudges its estimate toward it.
Two problems organize everything: prediction estimates the value of a fixed policy, and control searches for a good policy.
One distinction cuts across all of it: on-policy methods learn about the policy they act with, off-policy methods can learn about a different one.
The most direct way to estimate a value from experience is to take the definition literally and average actual returns.

visual.sub: Average the returns you actually saw

beats.text: After an episode ends, compute the return that followed each visit and move the estimate a fraction toward it.

visual.note: With alpha = 1/N(s) this is exactly the running sample mean.

beats.text: The target is the true observed return, with no model and no bootstrap standing in for it.
For an episode of rewards one, one, one with discount zero point nine, the Monte-Carlo target is two point seven one.
Monte-Carlo is unbiased and makes no Markov assumption, but it must wait for the episode to end and carries high variance.
Here is the idea the chapter is built around: substitute your current guess of the next state for the unknown future return.

visual.sub: Bootstrap a guess from a guess

beats.text: The target becomes one real reward plus the discounted estimate of the rest, so the agent can learn from a single step online.

visual.note: The bracket is the TD error: surprise between expectation and one step of reality.

beats.text: That bracket is the temporal-difference error, the gap between two successive predictions of the same return.
With reward zero, next-state value one, old estimate zero point five and discount one, the TD error is zero point five.
TD trades bias for lower variance: it leans on a guess but depends on only one reward and one transition.
Updating every step instead of at episode end lets TD work even in continuing tasks that never terminate.
Control needs action-values, because greedy selection from Q is trivial while you cannot act greedily from V without a model.

visual.sub: Off-policy control via the greedy bootstrap

beats.text: The update bootstraps from the best next action, regardless of what the agent actually does next.

visual.note: The max over next actions is what makes Q-learning off-policy.

beats.text: A single update with learning rate one half, reward one, and discount zero gives a new Q-value of zero point five.
Tabular Q-learning provably converges to the optimal Q-star when every pair is visited and the learning rate shrinks correctly.

visual.note: Robbins-Monro: alpha must shrink, but not too fast.

beats.text: The max operator picks overestimated values, so Q-learning tends to overestimate action-values until corrected.
Decoupling behavior from learning is exactly the property that later made deep Q-networks possible.
Change one symbol and you get SARSA: bootstrap from the action the agent actually takes next under its own policy.

visual.sub: Learn the value of the policy you follow

beats.text: The only change from Q-learning is replacing the max with the value of the action actually taken.

visual.note: SARSA learns the value of the policy it is actually following, exploration cost included.

beats.text: On the cliff-walking example Q-learning takes the optimal edge path while SARSA learns a safer route one row back.
Exploration is annealed: epsilon must stay positive for convergence but vanish so the final policy stops wasting reward.
With start one, floor zero point zero five and time constant five hundred, epsilon approaches its floor of zero point zero five.
Too fast a decay and the agent commits before it has seen enough, the most common silent failure in value-based RL.
